title: Quiz: Key Management in Quantum Networks

questions.question: What are the four main stages of the key lifecycle?
questions.explanation: The key lifecycle consists of Generation (QKD produces the key), Storage (secure storage with metadata), Use (encryption, authentication, etc.), and Retirement (secure deletion when no longer needed).

questions.question: Why should keys be retired with secure deletion?

options.text: To prevent recovery of old key material

questions.explanation: Secure deletion (zeroization) ensures that retired key material cannot be recovered, even if an attacker gains access to storage media. Simply deleting doesn't remove the data.

questions.question: What is the role of a Hardware Security Module (HSM) in key management?

options.text: Provide tamper-resistant key storage and operations

questions.explanation: HSMs provide tamper-resistant hardware for storing cryptographic keys and performing operations. They protect against physical attacks and ensure keys never exist in unprotected memory.

questions.question: In trusted relay key distribution, how is the end-to-end key created?

options.text: The relay sends the same key to both parties
XOR of segment keys from each QKD link

questions.explanation: In trusted relay, Alice shares K_AR with relay and relay shares K_RB with Bob. The relay XORs these to create K_AB = K_AR XOR K_RB, which both parties can compute.

questions.question: Why is key synchronization important between QKD peers?

options.text: To ensure both parties have the same keys and states

questions.explanation: Key synchronization ensures both parties agree on which keys exist, their states (valid, expired, retired), and usage counts. Desynchronization could cause communication failures or security issues.

questions.question: What metadata should be tracked for each quantum key?

options.text: Creation time, source QKD, security level, usage count, expiration
Just the peer name and key length
Only whether the key has been used

questions.explanation: Comprehensive metadata enables proper key lifecycle management: creation time for aging, source QKD for auditing, security level for access control, usage count for limits, and expiration for automatic retirement.

questions.question: Key confirmation hashes should contain the actual key material for verification.
questions.explanation: False. Key confirmation uses a hash of the key, not the key itself. Sending the actual key would defeat its purpose. The hash allows verification that both parties have the same key without revealing it.
